prior_learning: Students need to understand the concept as a fraction as part of a whole and be confident with describing fractions.
common_misconceptions: When students add or subtract fractions they add/subtract the denominators as well. They need to be able to convert to equivalent fractions with the same denominator before embarking on addition/subtraction where denominators are different.
Students will need lots of visuals to help them compare, convert or calculate with fractions before removing the visual. When students get stuck return to using visuals.
Students need to understand that fractions, decimals, percentages and ratio are all really the same concept but looked at in different contexts.

learning_outcomes: Re-writing fractions with a common denominator
key_knowledge_components: I know what equivalent fractions are.
I can identify equivalent fractions, simplify fractions, rewrite fractions over a common denominator and put fractions in order of size.

key_questions: Using the fraction wall, tell me a fraction equivalent to ?
How many fractions can you find equivalent to ?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start with a fraction wall so that students can see combinations of equivalent fractions Once students are secure with the concept of equivalence take away the fraction wall and teach them how to write equivalent fractions numerically, starting with fraction chains (see example in Key Questions) Initially students may need help identifying the common denominator (see example in key questions) Then move to comparing 2 fractions by writing with a …

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. If students don’t understand any concepts return to using visuals.  Only remove the visuals when you are confident they are secure.
notes.deepening_knowledge_summary: Give some questions for students to compare a fraction with a percentage/decimal such as: Which is bigger 75% or  ? Give more than 2 fractions for students to order eg: Put these fractions in ascending order Week/ Lesson Key Question?

key_questions: How do I know when I have fully simplified a fraction?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start where students are simplifying by dividing by 2/halving each time Move to examples where to simplify you need to divide by numbers other than 2 eg Then give students a generalization to help them simplify any given fraction: Check they are secure and then give examples top consolidate. Finish with probing questions to help them generalize (See Always/Sometimes/Never True questions under ‘key questions’)

Adding and subtracting fractions with different denominators
key_knowledge_components: I know the rules of addition and subtraction of fractions
I can apply the rules of addition and subtraction of fractions

key_vocab: Equivalent, numerator, denominator, ascending, descending, simplify, cancel-down,

teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start by adding/subtracting fractions with the same denominators. Once secure with fractions with the same denominator introduce different denominators so that they need to incorporate equivalent fractions. Once students can add/subtract fractions with different denominators give them non-routine questions such as whole numbers.

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Students often forget that you can only add/subtract fractions with the same denominators.  They also add both the numerators and denominators. Ask questions that expose this misconception. Return to using visuals any time a student doesn’t …

learning_outcomes: Converting improper fractions to mixed numbers and vice versa
key_knowledge_components: I know that mixed numbers and improper fractions are the same fraction written in different ways.
I can convert between mixed numbers add improper fractions.

key_vocab: Equivalent, numerator, denominator, ascending, descending, simplify, cancel-down, improper fraction, mixed number

key_questions: When would we want to write a fraction as an improper fraction?
When is it better to write a fraction as a mixed number?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start by using diagrams to teach students understand how to convert mixed numbers to improper fractions. Likewise for converting improper fractions to mixed numbers

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Be explicit that improper fraction and its equivalent mixed number are the same amount but sometimes it’s easier to calculate in one format versus the other.

key_knowledge_components: I know the rules of multiplying and dividing fractions
I can apply the rules of multiplying and dividing fractions

key_vocab: Equivalent, numerator, denominator, ascending, descending, simplify, cancel-down, improper fraction, mixed number

key_questions: How do I multiply these 2 fractions?
What would I do if I had 3 fractions to multiply together?
How could I make this calculation easier?
x
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Give them examples where they should simplify before multiplying eg x Once students are secure with fraction multiplication start to  look at fractions of quantities eg  x 12. Use unitary fractions only initially.  Be explicit that  x 12 is the same as  of 12 Move onto non-unitary fractions teaching them to find the unit fraction first and then multiply. For example, for  x 20, calculate  x 20 first and then multiply the answer by 4.

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Be explicit with students that when you are asked to find a fraction OF a quantity ‘of’ means multiply.

learning_outcomes: Writing a problem as a ratio
key_knowledge_components: I know how ratio links to fractions
I can simplify ratios and use ratios to solve problems.

teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start by establishing how we write a ratio – see the first key question. Students need to understand that the numbers of the ratio go in the same order as the words.  For example red to blue is 1:2, whereas blue to red is 2:1. The language is important. Give examples where all students need to do is read a problem and write it as a ratio. Then move to simplifying a ratio Start with visuals and then move to simplifying ratios without the visual

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Whenever a student does not understand revert to diagrams to help explain ratio questions.  Encourage them to draw diagrams to help themselves answer questions.

learning_outcomes: Calculating % of a quantity (mental methods and calculator)
key_knowledge_components: I know how to find a percentage change or an amount after a percentage change.
I can find a percentage change or an amount after a percentage change

key_questions: What fraction is the same as 50%? 25%, 75%, 10%, 1%, 12.5%
If you need to find 80% what other easy percentages could you use to help you? (10% x 8 or other)
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Link this into fractions.  Start with simple fraction to percentage conversions eg 50%, 25%, 75%, 10% Teach students that they can convert percentages to a fraction and calculate using fractions instead. Teach students that if they can find 50%, then they can find 75% by halving the answer to 50% and then adding both answers eg 75% = 50% + 25% Get students calculating simple percentages using mental methods as above. Finally move onto teaching p…

learning_outcomes: % reductions eg sale prices (mental methods and calculator)
key_knowledge_components: I know how to find a percentage change or an amount after a percentage change.
I can find a percentage change or an amount after a percentage change
I can find the original value of an item before a percentage change.

key_questions: See questions on multipliers under ‘suggested teaching activities’.
What multiplier would I use if I wanted to reduce a price by 20%? How do you know?
What about if I wanted to reduce a price by  2%?
If I got a 10% pay rise what multiplier would I multiply my current wage by?
If I got a 1% pay rise what multiplier would I multiply my current wage by?
My car has lost 20% of its value in the first year.  I bought it for £12500 so what would it be worth now?  How do you know? What calculation do I need to do on my calculator?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start with revision of multipliers Then for the remainder of this lesson focus on percentage decreases, in particular, sale prices

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Students often multiply by 1.3 when they want to increase a quantity by 3% instead of 1.03.  Give them lots of practice at this eg an increase of 30% is 1.3 and increase of 5% is 1.05.

key_questions: I have £3000 in a savings account and get 5% interest at the end of the year.  How do I calculate how much interest I’ll get? How much will I have in my bank account at the end of the year?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Use practical examples such as VAT
